name: Texas
examName: Texas Sales Agent Real Estate Examination

examFacts.notes: Administered by Pearson VUE for the Texas Real Estate Commission (TREC).

intro:
The Texas real estate sales agent exam is one of the tougher licensing tests in the country, and the state section is where first-time candidates most often stumble. The Texas Real Estate Commission (TREC) delivers it through Pearson VUE as two separate portions, an 80-question national section and a 40-question Texas section, for 120 scored questions across 240 minutes of testing time. You need 70 percent on each portion and the two are scored separately, so a strong national score won't rescue a weak state score. That gap on the Texas section is why Texas-specific preparation matters more here than another round of national review.

State-specific prep matters more here than almost anywhere else. The Texas section leans on the Real Estate License Act and TREC rules, the intermediary relationship Texas uses in place of dual agency, the promulgated contract forms a sales agent has to use rather than draft, the seller's disclosure notice, community property and Texas homestead protections, and special topics like the Deceptive Trade Practices Act and the Veterans Land Board. None of that looks like the material a candidate in a neighboring state studies. A generic real estate practice test won't cut it. You need Texas-specific practice questions.

Below are 20 free Texas real estate test questions and answers from RealReady's full Texas bank. Each tests a specific topic with the correct answer and a plain-English explanation under the card. Read each one, commit to an answer, then reveal. Treat it like a real TREC practice test, and the ones you miss show you exactly what to focus on next.

Get the RealReady app on iPhone, iPad, or Android for the rest of the Texas question bank. The full app includes short articles that walk you through the why behind each topic, detailed explanations on every question, a missed-question drill mode, and progress tracking that shows your per-category accuracy.

faq.q: How many questions are on the Texas real estate exam?
faq.a: The Texas Sales Agent Real Estate Examination has 120 scored questions, split into an 80-question national portion and a 40-question Texas portion. Pearson VUE also mixes in unscored pretest items, 5 on the national portion and 10 on the Texas portion, that don't count toward your result. You get 240 minutes in total, 150 minutes for the national portion and 90 minutes for the Texas portion.

faq.q: What's the passing score for the Texas real estate exam?
faq.a: You need 70 percent on each portion, and the two are scored separately. That works out to 56 of 80 correct on the national portion and 28 of 40 on the Texas portion. You have to pass both to get licensed, so a high score on one portion doesn't make up for falling short on the other.

faq.q: How much does the Texas real estate exam cost?
faq.a: Pearson VUE charges $43 to sit the sales agent exam, paid by card when you make your reservation. The fee is the same whether you take both portions together or retake a single portion, and it is non-refundable. Sales agent is the entry-level real estate license Texas issues.

faq.q: How long is the Texas real estate exam?
faq.a: 240 minutes in total, which is four hours. Pearson VUE gives you 150 minutes for the 80-question national portion and 90 minutes for the 40-question Texas portion. The exam ends automatically when your time expires, and you leave the test center with your official score in hand.

faq.q: Is the Texas real estate exam hard?
faq.a: Plenty of first-time candidates pass, but the exam earns its reputation and the Texas portion is where people stumble. The national portion is manageable for most candidates who actually studied. The state portion catches people off guard, because Texas's intermediary brokerage model, promulgated contract forms, and homestead and community property rules don't match what national prep material teaches. Honest practice on Texas-specific questions is usually the difference between passing the first time and paying to retake a portion.

faq.q: What's on the Texas real estate exam?
faq.a: The 80-question national portion covers general real estate principles like property characteristics and ownership, valuation and appraisal, contracts and agency, real estate practice, disclosures, financing and settlement, and real estate math. The 40-question Texas portion focuses on the duties and powers of the Texas Real Estate Commission, licensing and standards of conduct, agency and brokerage including the intermediary relationship, promulgated contracts and seller disclosure, and special topics like community property, homestead protections, the Deceptive Trade Practices Act, and the Veterans Land Board. The Texas material carries more weight than its share of questions suggests, since it's the part most candidates have never seen before.

faq.q: What's the best way to prepare for the Texas real estate exam?
faq.a: After you finish your 180 hours of qualifying real estate coursework, the highest-leverage move is grinding through Texas-specific practice questions with honest feedback on every miss. National practice tests help with the 80 national questions but won't catch you on Texas's intermediary rules, promulgated forms, and disclosure requirements. The RealReady app gives you the full Texas question bank, plus a missed-question mode and per-category progress tracking so you can drill your weak spots. Most users study 30 to 60 minutes a day for two to four weeks before sitting the exam.
